1: "Dead in your transgressions and sins" — the category of spiritual death before God is found in Jewish apocalyptic literature (4 Ezra 7:48) and in the language of covenant curse: the covenant partner who abandons the covenant enters the sphere of curse and death (Deut 28). Paul addresses the Gentile Ephesians as those who had no covenant standing before God, and thus no access to the covenant life. This is not biological death or Greek philosophical dissolution of the soul but the Jewish covenantal category of exclusion from God's life-giving presence.
2: "The ruler of the kingdom of the air" — Second Temple Jewish cosmology located demonic powers in the lower atmosphere between earth and the moon (cf. 1 Enoch 18-19; Testament of Solomon; Jubilees 10). The "air" (ἀήρ) was understood as the domain of spiritual forces hostile to God, which would have resonated with both Jewish and Gentile readers familiar with Greco-Roman demonology. "The spirit who is now at work in those who are disobedient" — a common Jewish designation for the adversarial cosmic power who animates human rebellion against God.
3: "Children of wrath" is a Hebraic idiom — literally "sons of wrath" (υἱοὶ τῆς ὀργῆς) — meaning those who belong to, or are destined for, wrath. The same construction appears at Qumran: "sons of light" and "sons of darkness" are categories of belonging, not biological descent. "Gratifying the cravings of our flesh" uses the sarx (flesh) language that Paul shares with the Qumran community's use of bāśār (flesh) for the sphere of human weakness and moral failure opposed to God's Spirit (cf. 1QS III-IV).
4: "But God, being rich in mercy" — the dramatic reversal introduced by "but God" (ὁ δὲ θεός) is the rhetorical pattern of Jewish lament and prayer: extended description of hopeless human condition followed by divine intervention. This pattern structures the Psalms (e.g., Ps 22) and Jewish confessional prayer (Ezra 9; Neh 9). "Rich in mercy" echoes the Jewish confession of God's character: abundant in hesed (covenant-love) and rahamim (merciful compassion) is how God describes himself at Sinai (Exodus 34:6) and how Israel prays to him throughout the OT.
5: "Made us alive together with Christ" (συνεζωοποίησεν) introduces a cluster of compound verbs with the prefix syn- ("together with") that characterizes Ephesians 2:5-6. Believers are co-quickened, co-raised, and co-seated with Christ. This inaugurated eschatology — the claim that believers already share in the resurrection life — is a Christian adaptation of Jewish resurrection hope, which expected resurrection only at the end of the age. The parenthetical "(it is by grace you have been saved)" echoes the refrain of Ephesians 2:8.
6: "Seated us with him in the heavenly realms in Christ Jesus" — the realized eschatology here is striking within Jewish categories. Jewish apocalyptic texts described the righteous being exalted to heaven after death or at the end of the age (cf. 1 Enoch 39; 4 Ezra 7); Paul presents this as already accomplished for believers in the present. The spatial claim — that believers' true location is in the heavenly realms even while they inhabit earthly bodies — is Ephesians' most distinctive theological move.
7: "The coming ages" — Jewish eschatology distinguished this age (olam hazeh) from the age to come (olam haba); Paul's "ages" (αἰῶνες) in the plural envisions multiple future epochs of divine generosity. The demonstration of God's grace toward the Ephesians is not merely for their benefit but a cosmic exhibit — the church as the display space for God's inexhaustible riches before the watching creation.
8: "By grace you have been saved through faith" — the contrast with "works" (Ephesians 2:9) is not a generic critique of moral effort but a pointed argument about the boundary markers of God's covenant community. In Second Temple Judaism, "works" could specifically denote the acts of Torah that distinguished Israel from the nations (circumcision, food laws, Sabbath, purity regulations). Paul's claim is that faith — not ethnic or ritual boundary markers — is now the sole criterion of inclusion in the covenant community. "This is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God" — the antecedent of "this" is disputed, but the broader point is clear: the entire salvation, not merely the faith, is God's gift.
9: "Not by works, so that no one can boast" — boasting (καύχησις) in covenantal privilege was a recognized feature of Second Temple Jewish piety: knowing the Torah, circumcision, ancestry from Abraham were grounds for confidence before God (cf. Romans 2:17-20). Paul's denial of boasting does not target Gentile self-congratulation but specifically the Jewish claim to covenantal superiority over Gentiles. In the new community, neither group has standing that the other lacks.
10: "We are God's handiwork" (ποίημα) — the term echoes creation vocabulary. Jewish and Hellenistic writers used poiēma for a work of divine or skilled craftsmanship. Paul's claim is that believers are God's new creation (cf. Isaiah 65:17; 2 Corinthians 5:17) — a claim rich with prophetic freight. The works "prepared in advance" echoes predestination language within a covenantal framework: God's purposes are not reactive but deliberate, established before the events themselves.
11: "Gentiles by birth… called 'the Uncircumcision'" — akrobystia (uncircumcision) was a term of social contempt in the Jewish world. The Maccabean crisis, in which some Jewish men attempted to reverse their circumcision to compete in Hellenistic gymnasia (1 Macc Ephesians 1:15), had made circumcision a boundary marker with intense emotional weight. The irony in Paul's phrase "made in the body by human hands" captures the Jewish polemic against idols (made by human hands) and applies it to circumcision — pointing to its merely external, outward character.
12: "Excluded from citizenship in Israel" — politeia (citizenship) is Roman political vocabulary for full membership in a civic body with its attendant rights and privileges. Gentiles were political outsiders to Israel's covenant community, with no claim on the Abrahamic, Sinaitic, or Davidic covenants ("the covenants of the promise" — Paul uses the plural deliberately). "Without hope and without God in the world" — the Gentile world had eschatological philosophies (Epicurean, Stoic) but no communal hope grounded in a divine promise to a covenant people. The Greek atheos (without God) was actually used as an insult against early Christians who refused to worship the gods — Paul reclaims it to describe the pre-Christian Gentile condition.
13: "Once far away have been brought near" — the rabbinic distinction between "far" (rahōq) and "near" (qārōv) was a technical category for Gentiles (far from God) and Jews (near, because possessing the covenant). The terminology appears in Isaiah 57:19, which Paul will cite via Ephesians 2:17. Paul's claim is that Christ has dissolved this foundational soteriological distinction: those who were structurally, categorically far are now brought near — not by becoming Jews, but by the blood of Christ.
14: "The dividing wall of hostility" — scholars debate two referents: (1) the soreg, the low stone balustrade in the Jerusalem Temple separating the Court of the Gentiles from the inner courts, with inscriptions warning Gentiles that death awaited those who crossed it (two such inscriptions have been found archaeologically, one now in Istanbul); or (2) the Torah itself, functioning as the social and legal barrier between Jew and Gentile. Paul may intend both simultaneously — the Temple's literal wall was itself a physical expression of the Torah's boundary-marking function.
15: "Abolishing in his flesh the law with its commands and regulations" — the "ordinances" (dogmata) were the specific legislative provisions of Torah that created and maintained the social barrier between Jews and Gentiles: food laws preventing shared meals, purity regulations governing contact, circumcision marking ethnic membership. Paul does not abolish the moral content of Torah but its function as a community-marking boundary. "To create in himself one new humanity" — a third entity, neither Jew nor Gentile, is Paul's striking claim; not Jewish absorption of Gentiles, not Gentile eclipsing of Jews, but a new creation in Christ.
16: "One body" — sōma (body) was also the standard Greek term for a corporate association, a collegium — one of the many voluntary associations (trade guilds, burial societies, religious groups) that organized civic life in Roman cities. Paul's "one body" subverts this familiar social category: the criterion for membership is neither trade, ethnicity, nor devotion to a patron deity, but union with Christ. The reconciliation to God "through the cross" — the cross as instrument of execution was the most shameful death possible in Roman estimation; Paul treats it as the mechanism of peace.
17: "Peace to you who were far away and peace to those who were near" — Paul cites or paraphrases Isaiah 57:19, which the rabbis read as addressing Gentiles (far) and Israel (near). The announcement of peace after judgment and exile was the herald's proclamation of Isaiah 52:7 — the messenger running ahead of the returning King to announce that war is over. Paul presents Christ as both the herald and the content of that peace.
18: "Access to the Father by one Spirit" — prosagōgē (access, audience) was the term for being formally presented into the presence of a monarch or patron — a significant privilege requiring an intermediary. In the Roman imperial court, direct access to the emperor was restricted and required a patron to introduce the petitioner. Paul's claim is that through Christ, by the one Spirit, both Jew and Gentile have this royal audience before God — without the ethnic or cultic intermediaries previously required. The tri-personal structure (through Christ, by the Spirit, to the Father) reflects an early Trinitarian pattern of prayer.
19: "No longer foreigners and strangers" — the terms paroikoi (resident aliens) and xenoi (foreign visitors) defined precise legal categories in Roman civic life: the paroikos lived permanently in a city but lacked full citizenship rights (no vote, restricted property ownership, limited legal protection); the xenos was a temporary visitor with even less standing. Paul declares that Gentile believers are now politeiai (fellow citizens) with full civic standing and oikeioi (household members) — not guests accommodated in the house but family, with the intimacy and inheritance that entails.
20: "Built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets" — in the context of Ephesians, "prophets" most likely refers to both OT prophets who anticipated the Gentile mission (Isaiah 49:6; Amos 9:12) and NT prophets active in early churches (cf. Ephesians 3:5; Ephesians 4:11). The cornerstone (akrogōniaios) was the critical architectural element placed first, whose precise positioning determined the alignment of the entire structure. Excavations of Hellenistic buildings confirm the cornerstone's structural and symbolic importance.
21: "A holy temple in the Lord" — the temple metaphor for the believing community appears also in 1 Corinthians 3:16-17 and 2 Corinthians 6:16. In Jewish theology, the temple was the unique locus of God's dwelling (the Shekinah). The loss of the first temple and the perceived inadequacy of the second (which lacked the ark, the Urim and Thummim, and the divine fire) generated a strong expectation of a future restored temple in which God would truly dwell again (Ezek 40-48; 11QT at Qumran). Paul presents the church as the fulfillment of this eschatological expectation — not a building but a community.
22: The Shekinah (divine presence) had departed from the first temple at its destruction (Ezekiel 10:18-19; Ephesians 11:22-23). Second Temple Judaism knew the second temple lacked the ark of the covenant and the visible cloud-glory. The prophetic hope was for God's presence to return in the messianic age (Ezekiel 43:1-5; Haggai 2:6-9). Paul declares this hope fulfilled: God's Spirit now inhabits the community of believers as his dwelling. The "being built together" (συνοικοδομεῖσθε) is a present passive — ongoing construction, not a finished state.
